results.title: A malicious user can steal money out of the vault and other users
results.reasoning: The reported issue is specific to mishandling rebasing tokens (e.g., AMPL): allowances and approvals are based on deposited amounts that later change due to rebases, enabling theft via transferAllowance/transferOut by exploiting the mismatch. The audit content does not mention rebasing tokens or balance changes over time. Items about fee-on-transfer behavior (e.g., #19, #22) and approval-reset issues (e.g., #15, #16, #26) are different root causes and fixes. The closest item (#20) concerns approving an untrusted router during migration, which is unrelated to rebasing and would be fixed by whitelisting/validating router addresses; that fix would not resolve rebasing-driven balance/allowance drift nor the transferOut path described. Therefore, the specific rebasing-token theft vulnerability was not detected.

results.title: ThorChain will be informed wrongly about the unsuccessful ETH transfers due to the incorrect events emissions
results.reasoning: The described issue is that when an ETH send in transferOut (and related V5 paths) fails and bounces back to the vault, the router still emits a TransferOut event with the original recipient (to), misleading ThorChain into believing the outbound succeeded. None of the audit findings identify this specific incorrect event emission on ETH send failure. While the report has related items about event spoofing (issue 13) and event amount mismatches for fee-on-transfer tokens (issue 19) or msg.value semantics (issue 27), these are different root causes and fixes. Fixing those would not correct the wrong 'to' address emission on bounce-back. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected.
